meta.title: Switch GPU Mode (VM ↔ LXC) | ProxMenux Documentation
meta.description: Move an already-assigned GPU between VM mode (vfio-pci) and LXC mode (native driver) on a Proxmox host. ProxMenux detects the current binding, prompts per-workload conflict policies, handles orphan audio cleanup, and rebuilds initramfs only when needed.

header.title: Switch GPU Mode (VM ↔ LXC)
header.description: Reassign a GPU that's already in use — flip it from VM passthrough to LXC sharing or the other way round. ProxMenux handles all the host-side binding changes (vfio.conf, driver blacklist, modules, initramfs) and offers a clean policy for every VM or LXC currently using the GPU, so the switch doesn't leave broken config behind.

intro.title: What this does
intro.body: A GPU on a Proxmox host lives in one of two modes: bound to vfio-pci (reserved for a VM) or bound to its native driver (i915 / amdgpu / nvidia) so the host + LXCs can share it. Switch GPU Mode flips between those two without you having to hand-edit vfio.conf, manage blacklists, or remember which VM / LXC lines point to the card. It also warns you cleanly if a workload still references the GPU so you don't end up with a broken VM at boot.

when.heading: When should I use this?
when.intro: Use this script when a GPU is already assigned and you want to move it:

rows.situation: GPU is free — never assigned. Want to give it to a VM.
GPU is free — never assigned. Want to give it to an LXC.

rows.situationRich: GPU is in a VM via vfio-pci, I want to use it from an LXC instead.

rows.situation: GPU is shared with an LXC, I want to dedicate it to a VM.
I just want to completely unbind a GPU from everything.
rows.use: Yes — pick the LXC-mode target and then detach manually.

prereqs.title: Before you start
prereqs.assigned: A GPU already assigned — either in a VM via VFIO or attached to at least one LXC. If you haven't assigned it yet, start from Add GPU to VM / LXC instead.
prereqs.iommu: IOMMU enabled on the host — only strictly required when switching to VM mode, but worth having on either way. The script warns if the kernel param is missing.
prereqs.reboot: Be OK with a reboot. Switching GPU bindings at the kernel level means the host regenerates initramfs and you reboot to apply. The script prompts at the end.
prereqs.knowList: Know which VMs / LXCs are using the GPU. The script will find them and ask what to do with each, but it's faster if you already know the list.

blocklist.title: Not all GPUs are safe to pass to a VM
blocklist.body: A small blocklist of GPU IDs is refused for VM mode due to known passthrough instability (e.g. Intel Arc A770 8086:5a84 / 8086:5a85). If the selected GPU matches, the script explains why and exits. Switching to LXC mode is always allowed.

running.heading: Running the script
running.body: Open ProxMenux on the host, go to Hardware: GPUs and Coral-TPU → Switch GPU Mode (VM ↔ LXC).
running.imageAlt: Menu entry for 'Switch GPU Mode (VM ↔ LXC)' inside Hardware: GPUs and Coral-TPU

howRuns.heading: How the script runs
howRuns.body: Two phases as usual: everything is collected and validated first, nothing is applied until you confirm at the end.

detect.title: Detect GPUs and their current binding
detect.body: The script scans every VGA / 3D / Display controller on the host and inspects /sys/bus/pci/devices/*/driver to find the current kernel driver. You'll see each GPU labelled with its name, PCI slot and current driver binding — so you can tell at a glance which mode it's in.
detect.imageAlt: GPU checklist showing each detected GPU with its current driver (vfio-pci / nvidia / amdgpu / i915) and PCI slot

pickGpu.title: Pick the GPU(s) to switch
pickGpu.body: Single GPU → auto-selected. Multiple GPUs → checklist. You can tick several, but they must all be in the same current mode — otherwise the script can't pick a target mode for the batch and you get a "mixed mode" warning asking you to narrow the selection.
pickGpu.tipBody: Useful when you're rebuilding a host: "All three NVIDIAs go to VM mode, then the iGPU goes back to LXC." Two runs, each with uniform target, much less friction than one-at-a-time.

direction.title: Review the proposed direction
direction.intro: Based on the current mode, the script proposes the opposite as target:
items: VM → LXC: unbind from vfio-pci, let the native driver (nvidia, amdgpu, i915) reclaim the card so LXCs can share it. On NVIDIA, the per-BDF entry is removed from /etc/udev/rules.d/10-proxmenux-vfio-bind.rules so the nvidia module reclaims the GPU after reboot.

LXC → VM: bind to vfio-pci so the card is free for VFIO passthrough to a single VM. On AMD / Intel this means blacklisting the native driver and setting options vfio-pci ids=…. On NVIDIA the nvidia module is not blacklisted — instead a per-BDF udev rule applies driver_override=vfio-pci only to the GPUs you select, so other NVIDIA GPUs on the host keep their nvidia driver.

conflict.title: Conflict policy per affected workload
conflict.body: The script scans every /etc/pve/lxc/*.conf and /etc/pve/qemu-server/*.conf looking for references to the GPU's PCI slot. For each affected workload you pick a policy:
conflict.keepEffect: pct set -onboot 0 (or qm set). GPU lines stay in the config.
conflict.keepWhen: You plan to come back to this VM/LXC once the GPU is back in its original mode. Safe default.
conflict.removeEffect: hostpci / dev lines for this GPU's slot are sed'd out.
conflict.removeWhen: The VM/LXC will keep running without the GPU (CPU-only transcoding, etc.). Clean workflow.
conflict.imageAlt: Dialog asking per-VM / per-LXC conflict policy when switching a GPU that's currently assigned

audio.title: Orphan audio cleanup (only when leaving VM mode)
audio.body1: dGPUs (NVIDIA / AMD) ship with an HDMI audio function at .1 of the same slot, and sometimes extra audio controllers are attached alongside the GPU. When the GPU leaves the VM, those audio lines become orphans — the VM has hostpci entries pointing to audio devices that aren't going with the GPU.
audio.body2: The script discovers them (precise BDF match, no substring false-positives) and shows a checklist so you can remove them cleanly. It also cleans their vendor:device IDs from /etc/modprobe.d/vfio.conf — but only if no other VM still uses those audio IDs.

apply.title: Apply host + workload changes
apply.body: Once you confirm, the script writes the host-side changes — vfio.conf, blacklist, modules, and (for NVIDIA) the per-BDF udev rule at /etc/udev/rules.d/10-proxmenux-vfio-bind.rules plus the BDF state at /etc/proxmenux/vfio-bind.bdfs. It also applies the chosen conflict policy to each affected VM/LXC. If the host config actually changed, it runs update-initramfs -u -k all — otherwise it skips that step.

reboot.title: Reboot
reboot.body: The new GPU binding only takes effect after a reboot. The script prompts you; you can reboot now or later, but don't start the target VM/LXC until the host has rebooted — otherwise the GPU is still held by the previous driver.
reboot.imageAlt: Summary dialog listing what changed, followed by the reboot prompt

manual.heading: Manual equivalent
manual.intro: If you want to understand exactly what the script does (or troubleshoot one of the steps by hand), these are the raw operations for VM → LXC on an NVIDIA card with vendor:device 10de:2204:
manual.oneVmTitle: Only one VM can use a given vfio-pci GPU at a time
manual.oneVmBody: Putting multiple hostpci entries with the same PCI slot in two VMs is valid config but only one of the VMs can start with the GPU — the second one will fail. The ProxMenux conflict policy step is exactly about avoiding this trap.

troubleshoot.heading: Troubleshooting
troubleshoot.stillVfioTitle: GPU still shows vfio-pci after switching to LXC mode
troubleshoot.stillVfioBody: update-initramfs didn't run (or the reboot didn't actually happen). Check lsmod | grep vfio — if vfio-pci is loaded, rerun update-initramfs -u -k all and reboot. For AMD/Intel: verify vfio.conf no longer contains the GPU's vendor:device ID. For NVIDIA: verify the BDF is no longer in /etc/proxmenux/vfio-bind.bdfs and that /etc/udev/rules.d/10-proxmenux-vfio-bind.rules doesn't list it.
troubleshoot.vmFailTitle: A VM won't start after switching a GPU to LXC mode
troubleshoot.vmFailBody: The VM still has hostpci entries pointing to a GPU it can't claim. Run the script again and pick the Remove GPU from config policy, or clean the config by hand:
troubleshoot.smiFailTitle: nvidia-smi fails with 'Driver/library version mismatch' after going back to LXC
troubleshoot.smiFailBody: Host NVIDIA modules didn't reload cleanly. modprobe -r nvidia then modprobe nvidia. If that fails, reboot — a full reboot always clears residual state from the vfio binding.
troubleshoot.logBody: Every run writes to /tmp/proxmenux_gpu_switch_mode.log on the host. Attach it when asking for help on GitHub.

items.label: Install NVIDIA Drivers (Host)
items.tail: — required for LXC mode on NVIDIA GPUs.

items.label: ProxMenux Monitor — Hardware tab
items.tail: — the Graphics Cards section where each GPU shows its current mode and exposes the inline switch control.
